title: Bajan chicken stew
subtitle: Barbadian stewed chicken with Bajan seasoning
excerpt: Chicken pieces marinated overnight in a paste of spring onion, thyme, garlic, and scotch bonnet, then browned and braised in a thick, dark sauce of tomato, onion, and browning sauce. Bajan chicken stew is close in spirit to Jamaican brown stew chicken but the Bajan seasoning (heavy on the spring onion, thyme, and marjoram) gives it a lighter, more herbal character. The sauce should be thick, dark, and coat every piece of chicken.
sourceNotes: Bajan stewed chicken is the everyday dinner dish of Barbados — found in homes, rum shops, and restaurants across the island. The Bajan green seasoning paste (spring onion, thyme, marjoram, garlic, scotch bonnet) is the defining flavour element that distinguishes it from other Caribbean stewed chicken recipes. The dish reflects the British-influenced stew cooking tradition that was prevalent in colonial Barbados.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well. The sauce deepens on reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Overnight marinade is preferred. Minimum 4 hours.

content.text: Bajan seasoning is the defining element: a paste of spring onion, fresh thyme, garlic, and scotch bonnet, blended or chopped fine. The seasoning goes on the chicken overnight so the flavours have time to penetrate. Without the overnight rest the chicken has less depth.

Browning sauce (available at Caribbean food shops) gives the sauce its dark colour and depth. A teaspoon is sufficient — more will make the sauce bitter. Dark soy sauce works as a substitute for colour, though the flavour is different.

Combine the Bajan seasoning ingredients ({{spring-onion}}, {{thyme}}, {{garlic}}, {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{black-pepper}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}) and rub into the {{chicken-thigh}} pieces. Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a large heavy pan over high heat. Brown the chicken pieces on all sides, about 4 minutes per side. Remove and set aside.

Add the {{onion}} to the pan and cook for 4 minutes. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and {{browning-sauce}}. Stir and scrape up the browned bits. Return the chicken. Add 100 ml of water, bring to a boil, then reduce to a medium simmer. Cover and cook for 25 minutes. Remove the lid and cook for a further 10 to 15 minutes until the sauce is thick, dark, and deeply coated on the chicken. Serve with rice and peas, macaroni pie, or sweet potato.

items.cause: Too much water or lid left on throughout
items.fix: Remove the lid for the final 15 minutes and increase the heat slightly. The sauce should reduce to a thick, dark gravy

items.cause: Overcooked or heat too high during the braise
items.fix: Bone-in thighs take 35 to 40 minutes in total. They are very forgiving, but 60 minutes will dry them out. Use medium heat for the braise

items.cause: Bajan seasoning underseasoned or not enough contact time
items.fix: Add a second teaspoon of browning sauce and an extra pinch of black pepper to the sauce. Season aggressively — Bajan stew should be deeply flavoured

content.text: Bajan chicken stew is the everyday dish of Barbados — playing the same role that Jamaican brown stew chicken plays in Jamaica. It is made in homes, sold at rum shops with rice and peas, and eaten on weekday evenings across the island. The Bajan flavour profile (heavy on thyme and spring onion, lighter on allspice than the Jamaican version) is quietly distinctive; you would recognise it as Barbadian rather than Jamaican or Trinidadian, though to most non-Caribbean palates the dishes appear similar. In Britain it is made in Barbadian households as a weeknight staple.
